narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and I know receiving this rejection must feel incredibly disheartening amid the unique challenges you may have faced. The admissions model evaluated your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, LSAT score of 36, Year 1 school decile of 2, Year 3 decile of 3, upper-middle family income, and full-time student status—against patterns from thousands of past students' paths to the bar exam. It predicted a 62.5% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try, which tipped the scales to reject your application as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factors pulling the prediction toward failure were your Year 3 grades, which placed you in the 3rd decile—lower than the typical student's 5.53 average—suggesting a dip that the model sees as a stronger warning sign for bar struggles. Right behind that, your Year 1 decile of 2, well below the average of 5.71, reinforced concerns about early academic momentum compared to peers who ranked higher. Your LSAT score of 36, slightly under the 36.7 average, actually worked in your favor by nudging the prediction toward passing, showing your potential in that area. Your upper-middle family income contributed somewhat toward the failure prediction, as the model links certain brackets to varying bar outcomes in its data. Your undergraduate GPA edged slightly toward failure risk, while your full-time status had minimal positive influence, aligning with the vast majority of students. Overall, your position below average in those key grade deciles set you apart from typical admits who cluster higher, outweighing your other strengths. This isn't a judgment on your worth or resilience—many overcome similar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your recent grades through targeted courses or tutoring, and reapplying next cycle with a narrative highlighting your growth as a Black woman trailblazer in law. You've got the drive; refining these areas can shift the odds dramatically in your favor. Keep pushing—you belong in this field.
